county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1853 from Contra Costa and Santa Clara counties. Named for the tree-lined creek (alameda means 'grove of poplars' in Spanish). Major cities include Oakland and Berkeley. UC Berkeley's Bancroft Library is a premier California research collection.
county_metadata.research_tips: Alameda County records begin 1853 at the County Recorder in Oakland. The Bancroft Library at UC Berkeley holds premier California manuscript collections including Spanish and Mexican era documents. Oakland Public Library's History Room has city directories from 1869. For pre-1853 records, check both Contra Costa and Santa Clara counties. FamilySearch has microfilmed Alameda County vital records and deeds. The Oakland Museum of California holds photographs and community records. Chinese community records in Oakland's Chinatown predate San Francisco's 1906 losses.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Index and images available through county recorder portal

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864, California's least populated county. Encompasses the Sierra Nevada crest. Mining communities of Silver Mountain and Monitor thrived briefly in the 1860s. Most early residents were miners and ranchers.
county_metadata.research_tips: Alpine County's tiny population means fewer records but also easier to locate specific families. County Recorder in Markleeville has deeds from 1864. Pre-1864 records split among Amador, El Dorado, Calaveras, Mono, and Tuolumne counties. Mining district records are critical for tracing Silver Mountain City and Monitor settlers. California State Archives in Sacramento holds some Alpine County records. The county courthouse has burned and been rebuilt; verify record survival dates with the county clerk. Census records from 1870 onward capture most families due to small population.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Markleeville — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1854 from Calaveras County. Named for Jose Maria Amador, a rancher and Gold Rush miner. Jackson and Sutter Creek were major Gold Rush towns. Kennedy and Argonaut mines operated into the 1940s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Amador County Recorder in Jackson holds deeds from 1854. The Amador County Archives and Museum in Jackson preserves mining-era records and photographs. Rich Gold Rush heritage means mining claim records at BLM are essential. Kennedy Mine records trace Italian immigrant miners. Pre-1854 records in Calaveras County. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Amador Ledger newspaper (from 1871) provides obituaries and legal notices. Check cemetery records in Jackson, Sutter Creek, and Ione for early families. Italian and Serbian immigrant communities well documented in local histories.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Jackson — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Named for the Sutter Buttes. Gold mining along the Feather River drew thousands in 1849-1850. Oroville was a major Chinese mining community. Bidwell family papers at CSU Chico are significant.
county_metadata.research_tips: Butte County is an original 1850 county with records from California's earliest statehood period. County Recorder in Oroville has deeds from 1850. CSU Chico's Meriam Library Special Collections holds Bidwell family papers and local history. Oroville's Chinese Temple (1863) reflects the large Chinese mining community. Check Butte County Historical Society for local family files. FamilySearch has microfilmed vital records and deeds. For Gold Rush miners, BLM land patent records and mining claim records are essential. The Oroville Mercury newspaper (from 1853) provides early obituaries and community notices.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Oroville — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Mark Twain set 'The Celebrated Jumping Frog' in Angels Camp. Rich Gold Rush history with mining camps at Mokelumne Hill, San Andreas, and Murphys. County archives date to 1850.
county_metadata.research_tips: Calaveras County Recorder in San Andreas holds records from 1850, California's statehood year. This is a premier Gold Rush county — mining camp records at Angels Camp, Mokelumne Hill, and Murphys are extensive. Mark Twain's famous story was set in Angels Camp. Calaveras County Historical Society preserves mining-era documents and photographs. BLM General Land Office records for mineral patents are essential. Pre-statehood Mexican-era records are scarce. FamilySearch microfilms include county records. Check Calaveras County Museum in San Andreas for family files and obituary indexes.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, San Andreas — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Agricultural region in the Sacramento Valley. Named for the Colusi people. Glenn County separated in 1891. Early records include Mexican-era land grants along the Sacramento River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Colusa County Recorder has deeds from 1850. An original county with agricultural heritage, its records include Mexican-era land grants along the Sacramento River. Glenn County separated in 1891, so post-1891 records for the northern portion are in Glenn County, Willows. Colusa County Free Library has local history holdings. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Colusa Sun newspaper provides obituaries and legal notices from the 1850s. Check for Native American (Colusi/Patwin) records through NARA and BIA files. Sacramento Valley agricultural census records supplement federal census data.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Colusa — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Name means 'opposite coast' (from San Francisco). Martinez was county seat from 1850. John Muir's home in Martinez is now a national historic site. Significant railroad and industrial history.
county_metadata.research_tips: Contra Costa County Recorder in Martinez has records from 1850, California's statehood. John Muir National Historic Site archives in Martinez hold naturalist's papers. Contra Costa County Historical Society has family files and photographs. The county's industrial history (Standard Oil in Richmond, C&H Sugar in Crockett) means company records and union records supplement genealogical research. FamilySearch has microfilmed vital records and deeds. Pre-statehood records relate to Rancho El Pinole and other Mexican land grants. Check Contra Costa Times newspaper for obituaries and community notices.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online index and document images from 1854

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1857 from Klamath County (which was abolished in 1874). California's northwesternmost county. Crescent City is the county seat. Tolowa and Yurok tribal lands are significant. Timber industry dominated from the 1850s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Del Norte County Recorder in Crescent City has deeds from 1857. Originally part of Klamath County (abolished 1874); pre-1857 records may be in Humboldt County. Del Norte Historical Society preserves lumber and fishing community records. Tolowa and Yurok tribal records are held by tribal governments and NARA Pacific Region. The 1964 tsunami destroyed some coastal records in Crescent City. Check Humboldt County for Klamath County records. FamilySearch microfilms include some county records. Timber industry payroll records trace families working in the redwood lumber mills.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Crescent City — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Named for the legendary city of gold. Coloma is where James Marshall discovered gold at Sutter's Mill on January 24, 1848, triggering the Gold Rush. Placerville was originally called Hangtown.
county_metadata.research_tips: El Dorado County Recorder in Placerville holds records from 1850 — this is where the Gold Rush began at Sutter's Mill in Coloma on January 24, 1848. The El Dorado County Museum in Placerville has Gold Rush-era artifacts and documents. Marshall Gold Discovery State Historic Park preserves the discovery site. Mining claim records at BLM are essential for tracing forty-niner families. Placerville was originally 'Hangtown' — early court records reflect frontier justice. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check the Mountain Democrat newspaper (from 1851) for early obituaries and legal notices.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Placerville — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1856 from parts of Mariposa, Merced, and Tulare counties. Central Valley agricultural powerhouse. Significant Armenian, Japanese, and Dust Bowl migrant populations. Fresno County Library has extensive local history collections.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fresno County Recorder has deeds from 1856. The Fresno County Library's Heritage Center has extensive local history holdings including the Fresno Bee newspaper morgue files. The Armenian Museum of Fresno documents the large Armenian community (arrived 1880s-1920s). Japanese families farmed extensively before WWII internment — check NARA records for Fresno Assembly Center. Dust Bowl migrants arrived in the 1930s — Sanborn maps and city directories trace their settlement. Pre-1856 records split among Mariposa, Merced, and Tulare counties. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online index; images for recent decades
